\chapter{Chapter 4}\label{appendix:C}
\index{Appendix@\emph{Appendix C}}

\section{Geometry of \texorpdfstring{$\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$}{Omega\_psi-hat} for Simpson's index}

Recall that for a model with full parameter $\symbf{\theta} \in \Theta$ and parameter of interest $\psi = \varphi(\symbf{\theta})$, the set
$$
\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \left\{\symbf{\omega} \in \Theta: \varphi(\symbf{\omega}) = \hat{\psi}\right\}
$$
contains all values of the parameter space that also get mapped to the MLE of $\psi$ under the interest function $\varphi$. In that sense, we can think of $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ as being the level set of $\varphi$ for some value of $\hat{\psi}$, i.e. a set of vectors in $\Theta$ that $\varphi$ maps to the same $\hat{\psi}$.

In the multinomial model with $J$ categories, the natural parameter space is the open probability simplex
$$
\Delta^{J-1} = \Bigg\{\symbf{\theta} \in \mathbbm{R}^J: \theta_j > 0,\ \sum_{j=1}^J \theta_j = 1\Bigg\}.
$$
The relevant level set for Simpson's index is therefore
$$
\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \left\{ \symbf{\theta} \in \Delta^{J-1} : \sum_{j=1}^J \theta_j^2 = \hat{\psi} \right\}.
$$ 
We characterize the geometry of $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ by first considering the cases $J = 2$ and $J = 3$ in detail before generalizing to arbitrary $J$. While these low-dimensional cases do not produce nuisance parameters of sufficient dimension for the integrated likelihood to outperform the profile likelihood in any meaningful way, they nevertheless provide useful geometric intuition that carries over cleanly to the general case.

\subsection{The Case $J = 2$}

When $J = 2$, the parameter space is the simplex $\Delta^1$, which is just the line
$\{ (\theta_1, \theta_2) : \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 1,\ \theta_1, \theta_2 \in (0,1) \}$.
Simpson's index reduces to
$$
D(\symbf{\theta}) = \theta_1^2 + (1-\theta_1)^2 = 2\theta_1^2 - 2\theta_1 + 1.
$$
Setting $D(\symbf{\theta}) = \hat{\psi}$ and solving the resulting quadratic gives
$$
\theta_1 = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{2\hat{\psi} - 1}}{2}.
$$
When $\hat{\psi} = 1/2$, we have $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \{(1/2, 1/2)\} = \{\hat{\symbf{\theta}}\}$, since the uniform distribution is simultaneously the unique element of $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ and the MLE for $\symbf{\theta}$. The integrated likelihood function under the ZSE parameterization evaluated at $\psi$ is obtained by integrating $L(b(\symbf{\omega}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi}))$ with respect to $\symbf{\omega}$ over $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$, where $b(\symbf{\omega}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi})$ denotes the maximizer of $\mathbbm{E}(\ell(\symbf{\theta});\, \symbf{\omega})$ subject to $D(\symbf{\theta}) = \psi$. Since $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ in this case contains only $\hat{\symbf{\theta}}$, this integral reduces to the single evaluation $L(b(\hat{\symbf{\theta}}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi}))$, where $b(\hat{\symbf{\theta}}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi})$ is the maximizer of $\sum_j \hat{\theta}_j \log \theta_j$ subject to $D(\symbf{\theta}) = \psi$. But this is precisely the profile likelihood maximizer as well. Thus, for the special case in which $J = 2$ and the observed counts in both categories are equal, the integrated likelihood under the ZSE parameterization reduces exactly to the profile likelihood. Consequently, any inferences drawn from the two will be identical in this
case.

When $\hat{\psi} > 1/2$, the discriminant is strictly positive and $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ consists of exactly two distinct points:
$$
\symbf{\omega}^{(1)} = \left( \frac{1 + \sqrt{2\hat{\psi}-1}}{2},\ \frac{1 - \sqrt{2\hat{\psi}-1}}{2} \right),
\qquad
\symbf{\omega}^{(2)} = \left( \frac{1 - \sqrt{2\hat{\psi}-1}}{2},\ \frac{1 + \sqrt{2\hat{\psi}-1}}{2} \right).
$$
These two points are related by swapping the two category probabilities, and they migrate toward the corners $(1, 0)$ and $(0, 1)$ as $\hat{\psi} \to 1$. The integrated likelihood at $\psi$ is simply the sum
$$
\bar{L}(\psi) = L\bigl(b(\symbf{\omega}^{(1)}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi})\bigr) + L\bigl(b(\symbf{\omega}^{(2)}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi})\bigr),
$$
up to an arbitrary multiplicative constant.

Since $\symbf{\omega}^{(1)} = \mathbf{1} - \symbf{\omega}^{(2)}$ and the constraint $D(\symbf{\theta}) = \psi$ is symmetric in $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, the maximizer $b(\symbf{\omega}^{(2)}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi})$ is simply $\mathbf{1} - b(\symbf{\omega}^{(1)}, \psi;\, \hat{\psi})$. Only one constrained optimization is therefore needed to obtain both maximizers. The likelihood must still be evaluated separately at each, however, since $\hat{\psi} > 1/2$ implies $\hat{\theta}_1 \neq \hat{\theta}_2$ and therefore $N_1 \neq N_2$, so $L(\symbf{\theta}) = \theta_1^{N_1}\theta_2^{N_2}$ is not
invariant under permutation of $\symbf{\theta}$.

While the integrated likelihood is not precisely identical to the profile likelihood in the case where $J=2$ and $\hat{\psi} > 1/2$, the two will tend to agree closely in practice. This is because $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ contains only two points, leaving little room for the integrated likelihood to differ from the profile likelihood through averaging over meaningfully distinct nuisance parameters. It is well-established that inferences based on the integrated likelihood only begin to outperform those of the profile likelihood when the dimension of the nuisance parameter is large [@berger1999; @DeBin2015], so this result is unsurprising.

\subsection{The Case $J = 3$}

When $J = 3$, the simplex $\Delta^2$ is an equilateral triangle embedded in the affine
plane $\Pi = \{ \symbf{x} \in \mathbbm{R}^3 : x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 1 \}$. The constraint
$D(\symbf{\theta}) = \hat{\psi}$ defines a sphere of radius $\sqrt{\hat{\psi}}$ centered at the origin in $\mathbbm{R}^3$. To characterize the geometry of $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$, it will be helpful to consider first the set of all points in $\Pi$ satisfying this constraint without yet imposing the positivity constraint $\symbf{\theta} > \mathbf{0}$:
$$
\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}} = \left\{ \symbf{x} \in \Pi : D(\symbf{x}) = \hat{\psi} \right\},
$$
so that $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}} \cap \Delta^2$. Any $\symbf{x} \in \Pi$ may be decomposed as 
$$
\symbf{x} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{v}, \qquad \mathbf{c} = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}\right),
$$
where $\mathbf{c}$ is the vector from the origin to the centroid of $\Delta^2$ and $\mathbf{v} = \symbf{x} - \mathbf{c}$ is the displacement from the centroid to $\symbf{x}$. Since $\mathbf{1} = (1,1,1)^\top$ is the normal vector of $\Pi$ and 
$$
\mathbf{1} \cdot \mathbf{v}
= \sum_{j=1} v_j
= \sum_{j=1}^3 (x_j - c_j)
= \sum_{j=1}^3 x_j - \sum_{j=1}^3 c_j
= 1 - 1 = 0,
$$
$\mathbf{v}$ is orthogonal to $\mathbf{1}$. Since $\mathbf{c}$ is parallel to $\mathbf{1}$, it follows that $\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ as well. For any $\symbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$, expanding $D(\symbf{x}) = \hat{\psi}$ using this decomposition gives
$$
\hat{\psi}
= \symbf{x} \cdot \symbf{x}
= (\mathbf{c} + \mathbf{v}) \cdot (\mathbf{c} + \mathbf{v})
= \|\mathbf{c}\|^2 + 2(\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{v}) + \|\mathbf{v}\|^2
= \frac{1}{3} + \|\mathbf{v}\|^2,
$$
and so $\|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \hat{\psi} - 1/3$. Every point in $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ therefore lies at the same distance $r = \sqrt{\hat{\psi} - 1/3}$ from $\mathbf{c}$, so $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ is a circle of radius $r$ centered at $\mathbf{c}$ embedded within $\Pi$.

The geometry of $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}} \cap \Delta^2$ depends on how much of $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ lies within $\Delta^2$, which is determined by whether $r$ exceeds the inradius of $\Delta^2$ --- the distance from $\mathbf{c}$ to the nearest edge. The simplex triangle has vertices at $\symbf{e}_1, \symbf{e}_2, \symbf{e}_3$, side length $\sqrt{2}$, and inradius
$$
r_{\mathrm{in}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{3}}{6} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}.
$$
Setting $r = r_{\mathrm{in}}$ gives the clipping threshold
$$
\sqrt{\hat{\psi} - \tfrac{1}{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \implies \hat{\psi} = \frac{1}{2}.
$$
When $\hat{\psi} < 1/2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ lies entirely within $\Delta^2$ and $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ is a complete circle. At $\hat{\psi} = 1/2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ is tangent to all three edges simultaneously, touching at $(1/2, 1/2, 0)$ and its permutations --- the uniform distributions over two of the three categories. When $\hat{\psi} > 1/2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ extends beyond $\Delta^2$ and $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}} \cap \Delta^2$ consists of three disjoint arcs, one near each vertex. By the three-fold rotational symmetry of the equilateral triangle, all three arcs are geometrically identical. Each is symmetric about the axis from $\mathbf{c}$ toward its corresponding vertex, and so subtends a total angle of $2\alpha$ at $\mathbf{c}$, where $\alpha$ is the angle between the axis and either boundary of the arc.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{files/Images/simplex-level-sets.png}
  \caption{%
    \textsc{Level Sets of Simpson's Index on the Probability Simplex $\Delta^2$.}
    The circle $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ of radius $r = \sqrt{\hat{\psi} - 1/3}$ centered at the centroid $\mathbf{c}$ and embedded in the affine plane $\Pi$ is shown for three values of $\hat{\psi}$. When $\hat{\psi} < 1/2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ lies entirely within $\Delta^2$ and $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ is a complete circle. At $\hat{\psi} = 1/2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ is tangent to all three edges of $\Delta^2$ simultaneously, touching at the open circles marking the uniform distributions over two categories. When $\hat{\psi} > 1/2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ extends beyond $\Delta^2$ and $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}} \cap \Delta^2$ consists of three disjoint arcs, one near each vertex. Each arc subtends an angle $2\alpha$ at $\mathbf{c}$, with the centroid-to-vertex line bisecting the arc symmetrically.
  }
  \label{fig:simplex-level-sets}
\end{figure}

To derive $\alpha$ explicitly, introduce an orthonormal basis for $\Pi$:
$$
\mathbf{u} = \frac{(2, -1, -1)}{\sqrt{6}}, \qquad \mathbf{w} = \frac{(0, 1, -1)}{\sqrt{2}},
$$
where $\mathbf{u}$ points from $\mathbf{c}$ toward $\symbf{e}_1$. A point on $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ at an angle $\gamma$ from $\mathbf{u}$ is given by
$$
\symbf{x}(\gamma) = \mathbf{c} + r(\cos\gamma\, \mathbf{u} + \sin\gamma\, \mathbf{w}),
$$
with components
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1(\gamma) &= \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{2r}{\sqrt{6}}\cos\gamma, \\
x_2(\gamma) &= \tfrac{1}{3} + r\!\left(\tfrac{\sin\gamma}{\sqrt{2}} - \tfrac{\cos\gamma}{\sqrt{6}}\right), \\
x_3(\gamma) &= \tfrac{1}{3} + r\!\left(-\tfrac{\sin\gamma}{\sqrt{2}} - \tfrac{\cos\gamma}{\sqrt{6}}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$
The arc near $\symbf{e}_1$ is bounded by the simplex edges $x_2 = 0$ and $x_3 = 0$, and runs from $\gamma = -\alpha$ to $\gamma = +\alpha$. Since $x_3(\gamma) = x_2(-\gamma)$, these two boundaries are symmetric about $\gamma = 0$, so it suffices to set $x_2(\alpha) = 0$. Rearranging gives
$$
\sin\!\left(\alpha - \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = -\frac{1}{r\sqrt{6}},
$$
which has a solution only when $r \geq 1/\sqrt{6}$, i.e.\ when $\hat{\psi} \geq 1/2$. Substituting $r = \sqrt{\hat{\psi} - 1/3}$ yields
$$
\alpha(\hat{\psi}) = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6(\hat{\psi} - 1/3)}}\right) - \frac{\pi}{6},
\qquad \hat{\psi} \in \left[\tfrac{1}{2}, 1\right).
$$
At $\hat{\psi} = 1/2$, the argument of $\arcsin$ equals $1$ and $\alpha = \pi/3$, so the three arcs together tile the full circle, consistent with $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}} = \mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ in this boundary case. As $\hat{\psi} \to 1$, $\alpha \to 0$ and each arc shrinks to a point at its vertex.

The permutation group $S_3$ acts on $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$ by permuting category labels. Its six elements decompose into two types: three cyclic permutations that map the arc near $\symbf{e}_j$ to the arc near $\symbf{e}_k$ for $j \neq k$, and three reflections that map each arc onto itself by reflecting it across the axis from $\mathbf{c}$ to its corresponding vertex, which is equivalent to swapping its two non-dominant category labels. Each orbit under this action therefore contains exactly six elements, one pair from each arc.

This structure can be exploited in the Monte Carlo approximation of the integrated likelihood. Rather than drawing $6R$ points independently from $\Omega_{\hat{\psi}}$, one instead draws $R$ independent base points from a single arc and expands each to its full orbit of six by applying all elements of $S_3$. The within-orbit expansion is deterministic, so the Monte Carlo variance is driven entirely by the $R$ independent base draws. At the same time, exact coverage of all three arcs is guaranteed for every orbit, which reduces variance relative to naive independent sampling by eliminating the possibility of draws clustering in one arc and undersampling another. 

Importantly, this procedure applies in both regimes: when $\hat{\psi} \geq 1/2$ the three arcs are separated by the simplex boundary, but when $\hat{\psi} < 1/2$ the circle is complete
and the three-fold symmetry of $S_3$ still partitions $\mathcal{C}_{\hat{\psi}}$ into three equal sectors of $2\pi/3$, one associated with each vertex. The sampling strategy is identical in both cases, with $\hat{\psi} = 1/2$ being the transition point where the sector boundaries coincide with the simplex edges. The resulting approximation is
$$
\bar{L}(\psi) \approx \frac{1}{6R} \sum_{i=1}^{R} \sum_{\sigma \in S_3}
L\!\left(b\!\left(\sigma(\symbf{\omega}^{(i)}), \psi;\, \hat{\psi}\right)\right),
$$
where $\symbf{\omega}^{(1)}, \ldots, \symbf{\omega}^{(R)}$ are drawn independently from a single arc.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection*{General $J$}

\subsubsection*{The Level Set as a Sphere of Radius $\sqrt{\hat{\psi} - 1/J}$}

The decomposition used in the $J = 3$ case extends immediately to arbitrary $J$. For
any $\mathbf{p} \in \Delta^{J-1}$, write $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{v}$ where
$\mathbf{c} = (1/J)\mathbf{1}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ lies in the $(J-1)$-dimensional
subspace $\{\mathbf{v} : \sum_j v_j = 0\}$ orthogonal to $\mathbf{1}$. The sphere
constraint $\|\mathbf{p}\|^2 = \hat{\psi}$ expands to
$$
\underbrace{\|\mathbf{c}\|^2}_{1/J} + \underbrace{2\mathbf{c}\cdot\mathbf{v}}_{0} + \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \hat{\psi},
$$
giving $\|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \hat{\psi} - 1/J$. Every point on the level set lies at
distance $r = \sqrt{\hat{\psi} - 1/J}$ from the centroid within the simplex hyperplane.
The level set $\mathcal{L}(\hat{\psi})$ is therefore a \textbf{$(J-2)$-sphere of radius
$r$} centered at $\mathbf{c}$, intersected with the positive orthant of the simplex
hyperplane. In particular, $\hat{\psi} \geq 1/J$ is required for the level set to be
non-empty, with equality only at the uniform distribution $\mathbf{c}$ itself.

\subsubsection*{The Clipping Threshold}

The inradius of the $(J-1)$-simplex --- the distance from the centroid to the nearest
facet, measured within the simplex hyperplane --- is
$$
r_{\mathrm{in}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{J(J-1)}}.
$$
Setting $r = r_{\mathrm{in}}$ gives the clipping threshold
$$
\sqrt{\hat{\psi} - \frac{1}{J}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{J(J-1)}}
\implies
\hat{\psi} = \frac{1}{J} + \frac{1}{J(J-1)} = \frac{1}{J-1}.
$$
The three geometric regimes are therefore:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\hat{\psi} \in (1/J,\ 1/(J-1))$: the sphere fits entirely within the simplex
    interior and $\mathcal{L}(\hat{\psi})$ is a single connected $(J-2)$-sphere.

  \item $\hat{\psi} = 1/(J-1)$: the sphere is simultaneously tangent to all $J$ facets.
    The $J$ tangency points are the centroids of the facets, corresponding to the
    uniform distributions over $J-1$ categories, $(1/(J-1), \ldots, 1/(J-1), 0)$ and
    its permutations. This is a topological transition point where the level set is
    connected but pinches at $J$ boundary points.

  \item $\hat{\psi} \in (1/(J-1),\ 1)$: the sphere clips through all $J$ facets and
    the level set breaks into $J$ \textbf{disconnected components} (spherical caps),
    one near each vertex of the simplex. Each cap is a $(J-2)$-dimensional spherical
    cap of angular radius $\alpha(\hat{\psi}, J)$ centered on the axis from $\mathbf{c}$
    toward the corresponding vertex.
\end{itemize}
The threshold $\hat{\psi} = 1/(J-1)$ is a genuine \textbf{topological phase transition}:
below it the level set is connected; above it, it is not.

\subsubsection*{Angular Radius of the Caps}

For $\hat{\psi} > 1/(J-1)$, the angular radius of each cap is found by locating the
closest boundary point on each cap's edge. The boundary of the cap near vertex
$\mathbf{e}_1$ closest to the cap center is the point where $p_2 = 0$ and
$p_3 = \cdots = p_J$. Solving the resulting system gives the dominant category
probability at this boundary point:
$$
x_{\max} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{(J-2)\bigl((J-1)\hat{\psi} - 1\bigr)}}{J-1},
$$
and the angular radius of the cap is
$$
\boxed{\alpha(\hat{\psi}, J) = \arccos\!\left( \frac{1 + J\sqrt{(J-2)\bigl((J-1)\hat{\psi}-1\bigr)}}{(J-1)\sqrt{(J-1)(J\hat{\psi}-1)}} \right).}
$$
The boundary cases verify correctly: at $\hat{\psi} = 1/(J-1)$, $\alpha$ equals
$\arccos(1/(J-1))$ (for $J = 3$ this is $60°$); as $\hat{\psi} \to 1$, $\alpha \to 0$
and each cap collapses to its vertex.

\subsubsection*{Permutation Symmetry}

The full symmetric group $S_J$ acts on $\mathcal{L}(\hat{\psi})$ by permuting category
labels. The $J!$ elements decompose as:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textit{Between-cap permutations}: the $J$ permutations that cycle which
    category is dominant, mapping cap $j$ to cap $k$ for $j \neq k$.
  \item \textit{Within-cap permutations}: for each cap, the $(J-1)!$ permutations of
    the non-dominant categories, which map the cap onto itself.
\end{itemize}
This gives $J \times (J-1)! = J!$ total permutations, consistent with the size of
$S_J$. The minimal fundamental domain for sampling from the full level set is therefore
a region of angular extent $\alpha$ within a single cap, further reduced by a factor of
$(J-1)!$ due to the internal symmetry, with the remaining $J - 1$ caps recovered by
applying the $J$ between-cap permutations.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection*{Implications for Omega-Hat Sampling}

The geometry above directly motivates the following sampling strategy.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Regime 1: $\hat{\psi} < 1/(J-1)$ (connected level set).}
Sample a random direction $\mathbf{u}$ uniformly on the $(J-2)$-sphere within the
simplex hyperplane by drawing $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, I_J)$,
projecting out the $\mathbf{1}$ component, and normalizing. Set
$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{c} + r\mathbf{u}$ and accept if $p_j > 0$ for all $j$.
Acceptance probability is high since the sphere lies within the simplex interior.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Regime 2: $\hat{\psi} > 1/(J-1)$ (disconnected caps).}
Sample a direction $\mathbf{u}$ constrained to the cap near vertex $\mathbf{e}_1$,
i.e.\ within angular distance $\alpha(\hat{\psi}, J)$ of the unit axis
$\mathbf{n}_1 = (\mathbf{e}_1 - \mathbf{c}) / \|\mathbf{e}_1 - \mathbf{c}\|$.
Set $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{c} + r\mathbf{u}$. Then generate all $J$ between-cap
permutations of $\mathbf{p}$ to obtain one omega-hat candidate in each of the $J$ caps
simultaneously.

\medskip
In both regimes, each candidate $\mathbf{p}$ is mapped to logit space via
$\omega_j = \log(p_j / p_J)$ for $j = 1, \ldots, J-1$ to produce the omega-hat in the
parameterization used by \textit{likelyr}. This approach requires no numerical
optimization or constraint projection, provides guaranteed coverage of all $J$ caps in
the disconnected regime, and produces $J$ omega-hat candidates per sampled direction at
no additional computational cost.
